title: SAC Combustor Design
text: American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics 1 Design procedures and a Developed Computer Code for Preliminary Single Annular Combustor Design B. S. Mohammad1 and S. M. Jeng2 University of Cincinnati, Cincinnati, OH, 45220 Gas Turbine Combustor (GTC) design is a challenging and time consuming process. Several methodologies for conventional combustor design have been developed. The objective of this paper is to provide detailed procedures for the preliminary design of a Single Annular Combustor (SAC) (also known as Fuel Rich Dome Combustor). The design algorithm employs empirical and semi-empirical models which include diffuser section design, air distribution computations, combustor sizing, fuel nozzle design, axial swirler design, heat transfer calculations (with/without cooling, thermal barrier coating) and dilution holes design. The design process will provide the combustor geometry as well as an evaluation of the combustor performance (NOx and CO emissions). A computer code has been developed based on the design procedures. A SAC design has been implemented by the computer program in order to show the program capabilities. The program run time is in the order of magnitude of minutes. Our aim is to provide a tool to serve in educational purposes. Also, to provide a kit to help understand the important technical issues governing the GTC design. This paper shows the first version of the software. In future, the software will have more capabilities including the Lean Premixed Prevaporized (LPP) concept. Nomenclature 3.0 A = Pre-Diffuser inlet area 3.1 A = Pre-Diffuser exit area d A = Dome area AF =

Introduction He design process of a Fuel Rich Dome Combustor (also referred to as Single Annular Combustor (SAC)) is a challenging task. The design procedures involve lots of compute-intensive calculations. The design process includes coupled non-linear equations, implicit equations, different charts and lots of mathematics and logic to synchronize the design process. Mellor1 and Lefebvre2 collected an extensive list of empirical and analytical tools. They discussed details about the assumptions behind different equations involved in the design process as well as their range of validity. However, they did not provide a method to automate their design process. There are several other published preliminary designs for aircraft Gas Turbine Combustor (GTC).3-5 This paper presents a mechanism to

title: SAC Combustor Design
text: details about the assumptions behind different equations involved in the design process as well as their range of validity. However, they did not provide a method to automate their design process. There are several other published preliminary designs for aircraft Gas Turbine Combustor (GTC).3-5 This paper presents a mechanism to automate the design procedures of a SAC in a detailed step-by-step manner. An integral part of our automation mechanism, is the development of software that automates the design process. The fundamental objective of this software is to minimize the time spent in the preliminary aircraft GTC design process. The software will also enable the designer to fine tune several critical parameters and check their impact on the combustor’s size and performance in a timely manner. In Section II, the detailed design procedures of the SAC are presented. In Section III, the performance evaluation of the SAC will be discussed, while in the Section 3, the automation code is used to design a SAC. The performance of that specific SAC is also analyzed in Section 3. The reader is encouraged to refer to the books by Mellor1 and Lefebvre2 for more details on the assumptions and derivations of the design equations. II. SAC Design Procedures A. Data required to initiate the design process The design process assumes the following parameters are given: total m , t3 T , t3 P , cr , 3 M , cooling C , t4 T and c. The previous parameters are related to the thermodynamic

title: SAC Combustor Design
text: II. SAC Design Procedures A. Data required to initiate the design process The design process assumes the following parameters are given: total m , t3 T , t3 P , cr , 3 M , cooling C , t4 T and c. The previous parameters are related to the thermodynamic cycle and engine configuration. In other words, they represent the operational requirements and duty of the GTC. The numerical values are given at the point of T American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics 7 maximum power. Furthermore, in the current version of the design code, we assume a constant area combustor. The default input fuel is assumed to be Jet A. However, the user can simply change the properties to an alternate fuel. B. Air Distribution through the Combustor A typical GTC is shown in Fig. 1.6 The air flows from the compressor to two different combustor sections: the dome ( c _ d m ) and the passage ( c _ passage m ). The air flowing through the dome has three different purposes. The first is to atomize the liquid fuel issuing from the fuel nozzle ( c _ a m ). The second is to create a swirling flow that stabilizes the combustion ( c _ s m ) and the third to cool the dome ( c _ dc m ). Also, the air flowing through the passage has three purposes. The first is to provide cooling for the liner. The second is to provide the

title: SAC Combustor Design
text: swirling flow that stabilizes the combustion ( c _ s m ) and the third to cool the dome ( c _ dc m ). Also, the air flowing through the passage has three purposes. The first is to provide cooling for the liner. The second is to provide the primary air jets needed to complete the combustion reaction in the secondary region ( c _ p m ) and the third is to provide the dilution air jets necessary to achieve the required exit temperature distribution ( c _ dil m ). Usually 20-40 % of the air flows through the dome and the remaining through the passage. C. Combustor Sizing The main dimensions of the Fuel Rich Dome Combustor are shown in Fig. 2. We need to determine different geometrical variables as well as the pressure drop across the liner and the swirlers. The combustor main dimensions are estimated according to the velocity method. That is the dimensions will assume a certain prescribed velocity. Typical velocity ranges are assumed according to conventional combustor designs. Consequently, all the previously calculated flow rates could be obtained per nozzle. D. Pre-Diffuser Design Typically the pre-diffuser begins with a constant area section to dissipate the wakes. The constant area section is followed by a diffusing section to recover a good portion of the inlet dynamic head. The diffuser schematic is shown in Fig. 3.1 Dump diffuser follows the diffusing section to further reduce the speed and to enable stable combustion. The dump diffuser is a diffuser with large area expansion and minimum axial length that will result

title: SAC Combustor Design
text: recover a good portion of the inlet dynamic head. The diffuser schematic is shown in Fig. 3.1 Dump diffuser follows the diffusing section to further reduce the speed and to enable stable combustion. The dump diffuser is a diffuser with large area expansion and minimum axial length that will result in abrupt reduction of the flow velocity, but with a very slight pressure recovery. The geometrical variables of the pre- diffuser are given in Fig. 4. An important parameter to define is AR . It is the ratio of 3.1 3.0 A / A . The second parameter we define is DPTP . It is the ratio of the dynamic pressure to total pressure as given by Eq. (24). At the diffuser inlet the DPTP is usually around 6-8% and may even go to as high as 10%.1 The DPTP indicates the maximum available pressure to recover. At the exit of the pre-diffuser the DPTP is around 1.5%. Therefore, we are not worried about the total pressure loss that takes place in the dump diffuser (the maximum available is already 1.5%). American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics 11 Another important parameter to define is the p C . It measures how much pressure is recovered in the diffuser. The definition of p C is given in Eq. (25) and the pi C is given in Eq. (26). The difference between p C and pi C is that the ideal pressure recovery assumes that there are no losses in the passage.

One of the best approaches to the 2D diffuser is to obtain the total pressure loss in both the pre-diffuser (aerodynamics diffuser) and dump diffuser as function of AR . Then, the AR corresponding to the minimum pressure loss can be calculated. However, to automate the process we need to fit some curves for the 2D diffuser. Two fits are required for this process automation. First, the value of the length to width ratio ( LW ) that corresponds to the value of AR for no appreciable stall is required. This can be obtained from the chart provided by Renau et al9. Equation (27) shows the required relation for AR 2  while Eq. (28) is valid for AR >2. From the same chart

title: SAC Combustor Design

Fuel Nozzle design The fuel nozzle is responsible for delivering the required fuel flow rate at different flight conditions. The fuel provided should be atomized to a suitable diameter to enhance the fuel/air mixing. A Dual orifice fuel nozzle shall be designed. A typical dual orifice nozzle is shown in Fig. 5. The dual fuel nozzle is important because no single fuel nozzle will be able to deliver the engine requirements at different missions. The flow rate of the fuel is proportional to the square root of the differential pressure. Therefore, an increase in the flow rate by a factor of 40 requires an increase in the differential pressure by 1600 times. However, designing a pump with such a wide range of operation is not practical. The pilot nozzle is open for low pressures and once the pressure reaches set P  , the main nozzle also opens. There is also a min P  below which the pilot nozzle is closed (roughly 30 psi). There are minimum pressure requirements to achieve good atomization of fuel. The presence of a swirler in each fuel

title: SAC Combustor Design
text: and once the pressure reaches set P  , the main nozzle also opens. There is also a min P  below which the pilot nozzle is closed (roughly 30 psi). There are minimum pressure requirements to achieve good atomization of fuel. The presence of a swirler in each fuel circuit is important to impart a tangential component of velocity before the fuel exits the nozzle. As the fuel exits and because of the centrifugal effects, a spray in the form of a cone is formed from both the pilot and main nozzles. The main dimensions and performance of the fuel nozzle are estimated as follows: 1) We assume that max P  is given. 2) Assume suitable values of set P  and min P  . The fuel nozzle performance curve should look reasonable. This can be checked after plotting the nozzle performance curve. American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics 14 3) We assume that the fp max m at the point where the secondary valve opens is known. It is assumed as a fraction of the maximum fuel flow rate as depicted in Eq. (41). A fraction of 20% should result in a reasonable performance curve. fp max f _ pn m fr * m    (41) 4) dp C and ds C for both nozzles are assumed. Axial Swirlers Design The swirling flow is an essential feature of all modern GTC. A swirler imparts a swirl to the air before its admittance to the combustion dome. Consequently, a Central Recirculation Zone (CRZ) is set up. The CRZ is essential for three reasons. First, it is essential for anchoring the flame. The reduced flow velocities result in a flame that is stable and anchored close to the swirler exit. Second, the mixing between air and fuel is enhanced significantly. Third, it acts as a continuous source of ignition because a part of the

title: SAC Combustor Design
text: First, it is essential for anchoring the flame. The reduced flow velocities result in a flame that is stable and anchored close to the swirler exit. Second, the mixing between air and fuel is enhanced significantly. Third, it acts as a continuous source of ignition because a part of the combustion gases is mixed with the incoming air/fuel mixture and ignites it. Different swirler configurations are used in Gas Turbine Engines. A Typical co-axial swirler is shown in Fig. 6.12 Two axial swirlers are to be designed. Each one has a hub diameter and a tip diameter. The primary swirler will provide the air necessary for the atomization process. It will have a hub diameter equals to the secondary nozzle swirler chamber diameter (assumed 3 times the outer diameter). The secondary swirler will provide the secondary swirling air. The hub diameter of the secondary swirler will be the tip diameter of the primary swirler (assuming zero thickness). Fig. 7 shows a schematic of the swirlers/fuel nozzle assembly. Now we summarize the design procedures for an axial swirler: 1) v is assumed within 30-60.1 2) tv is assumed within 0.7-1.5 mm.1 3) vn is assumed within 8-16.1 4) v  is assumed around 1.0. 5) sw A is estimated using Eq. (50).13 It is reasonable to assume that lA is equal to d A . Also, one should notice that pdl is approximately equal to the swirler pressure drop. sw k has a value of 1.3 for flat vanes. sw m

Heat Transfer and Film Cooling Calculations The hot gases temperature in a GTC can be higher than 2000 C (3632 F). This will result in immediate melting of the liner. Thus, liner cooling is a must in all GTC. Frequently, the cooling is achieved using film cooling. The air film reduces the liner wall temperature and protects it from damage. Fig. 8 shows a schematic of a cooling strip. Our calculations start by assuming the liner is not cooled. Fig. 9 shows different heat transfer mechanisms involved in the gas turbine combustor.2 We notice that on the hot side convection takes place between the hot gases and the liner. Also, radiation exchange takes place between the hot gases and the liner. The net heat is then conducted through the liner to the other side. Heat transfer by convection takes place between the cold side of the

title: SAC Combustor Design
text: the hot side convection takes place between the hot gases and the liner. Also, radiation exchange takes place between the hot gases and the liner. The net heat is then conducted through the liner to the other side. Heat transfer by convection takes place between the cold side of the liner and the air flowing in the passage. Finally, heat transfer by radiation takes place between the liner and the casing. All formulas for different quantities will be introduced shortly. When we consider the film cooling, the internal convection ( 1 C ) will have a different formula. The reason being that the heat transfer takes place by convection between the hot gases and the effective film (temperature called effective film temperature or adiabatic wall temperature). Dilution Hole Design The temperature in the dome of a fuel rich combustor is very high and may potentially result in an immediate damage to the Gas Turbine blades if admitted directly. Also, since the combustion process in the primary zone is incomplete, dilution air is needed to complete the process and reduce the temperature of the gases. American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics 20 The dilution holes are usually designed to allow for maximum penetration of the dilution jet. Typically, the dilution jet will penetrate up to 40% of the dome height.2 An example of a jet issuing from a dilution hole is given in Fig. 10 where  is the jet’s angle, Y is the penetration and j d is the jet diameter. The design procedures of a simple round dilution holes are summarized as follows: 1) The jet velocity ( j U ) is calculated from Eq. (77). However, we will assume twice the number of big holes per fuel nozzle. III. Performance Evaluation Since the main dimensions of the SAC have now been estimated, performance evaluation is the next step. The parameters involved in the evaluation process are: the fuel spray Souter Mean Diameter ( SMD ), droplet life time ( dt , time required for complete evaporation), combustor residence time ( rt ), and NOx and CO emissions. The SMD of the fuel spray is a good indication of the droplet evaporation characteristics. The droplet lifetime needs to be estimated and compared to the available residence time in the combustor. The life time must be less than the available residence time. Otherwise, fuel droplets would emerge from the combustor and cause low combustion American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics 22 efficiency and high unburnt hydrocarbons emissions. There are stringent regulations on

title: SAC Combustor Design
text: compared to the available residence time in the combustor. The life time must be less than the available residence time. Otherwise, fuel droplets would emerge from the combustor and cause low combustion American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics 22 efficiency and high unburnt hydrocarbons emissions. There are stringent regulations on the amounts of NOx and CO and we will use the empirical formulas for their estimation. We also use the Zeldovich mechanism to solve for the NOx production rates. The combustor performance evaluation is carried out as follows: 1. The SMD for the primary and secondary fuel jets is estimated using Eq. (87)22 where f is the surface tension of the fuel and f  is the viscosity of the fuel. To include the air blast effect (account for atomizing air) we use Eq. (88)2. r U is the relative velocity between the air and fuel (m/s). The combustor is divided into three zones. Total flow rate is split into three parts representing the flow rate in each zone. For each region the temperature as well as the thermal properties are obtained from the NASA thermal equilibrium program at the corresponding equivalence ratio.16 Now we can get an estimate of the dt in each zone according to the procedures suggested by Turns23: The transfer number ( q B ) is estimated using Eq. (89) where th FA is the stoichiometric fuel air ratio by mass, fg h is the latent heat of vaporization of the fuel droplet and pfv C is the specific heat of the gaseous mixture (obtained from NASA program) . The Reynolds number based on the droplet diameter ( d Re ) is calculated from Eq. (90) where r V is the relative velocity between the fuel droplet and the combustion products. We will assume the r V to be 0.3 of the g U . The fuel droplet diameter ( f D ) is the SMD estimated previously. The Nusselt

title: SAC Combustor Design
text: ) is calculated from Eq. (90) where r V is the relative velocity between the fuel droplet and the combustion products. We will assume the r V to be 0.3 of the g U . The fuel droplet diameter ( f D ) is the SMD estimated previously. The Nusselt number ( Nu ) number is then computed in Eq. (91). The evaporation constant ( v K ) is estimated using Eq. (92). fv k is the gaseous mixture thermal conductivity (from NASA program). Finally, the dt is estimated from Eq. (93). If all the previous variables are substituted with the numerical values corresponding to the primary zone, an estimate of the lifetime based on the primary zone conditions can be obtained: American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics 23 .     g pfv g s th fg B C T T LHV / FA / h    (89) d g r g Re V D /    (90)     1/ 2 1/ 2 1/ 3 1/ 2 4 / 3 d g d g Nu 2 0.555 Re Pr / 1 1.232 / Re Pr    (91)     v fv f pfv g K 4k Nu / C ln B 1    (92) 2 d v t D / K  (93) 3. We also assume that the combustor is length is divided into three equal parts such that the each zone length ( zone L ) is just cL /3. The gas velocity in each zone ( zone V ) is estimated using the flow rate ( m / A   ). A simple estimate of the residence time in each region is then easily obtained using Eq. (94). The total residence time is the sum of the three region residence times. If the residence time in the primary zone is less than the fuel droplet evaporation time, step 2 should be repeated in the secondary region while using the D-squared law to get the new particle SMD . r zone zone t L / V  (94) 4. Another way to estimate the NOx (thermal only) is by using the Zeldovich mechanism25, 26 and the extended Zeldovidh mechanism.27 The equations can be solved approximately to provide a rough value for the NOx . The American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics 24 equations and the reaction rates are summarized in several references.12-17 The three equations responsible for the formation of the thermal NOx are Eq. (98) to (100). The forward ( f K ) and backward ( b K ) reaction rate constants for the three reactions are given by Eq. (101) to (106). The mole fraction of oxygen ( O2 ), nitrogen ( N2 ), monatomic oxygen ( O ), water vapor ( H2O ), hydroxyl (OH ) and monatomic hydrogen ( H

title: SAC Combustor Design
text: and backward ( b K ) reaction rate constants for the three reactions are given by Eq. (101) to (106). The mole fraction of oxygen ( O2 ), nitrogen ( N2 ), monatomic oxygen ( O ), water vapor ( H2O ), hydroxyl (OH ) and monatomic hydrogen ( H ) must be known for the three zones inside the combustor. A good approximation could be obtained from the NASA program assuming they are in thermal equilibrium. The mole fraction is then converted into molar concentration using Eq. (107). ix represents the mole fraction of the species i where i N represents the molar concentration of the species i and zP and zT are the pressure and temperature of the specific zone considered. c R is constant of conversion=82.05   3 cm .atm / gmol.K .We also assume that the monatomic nitrogen ( N ) is in equilibrium. Using this information, the N molar concentration ( N N ) is evaluated using Eq. (108) and the rate of generation of NO ( dNO / dt ) is given by Eq. (109). Equations (108) and (109) are solved in each zone. The total integration time is the residence time. The NO concentration is computed in ppm from Eq. (110). The previous procedures are repeated to obtain the NO concentration in all three zones. Finally, the total NO ( total NO ) generated in the combustor is estimated using Eq. (111). The rate of NOx in g/s is obtained from Eq.

title: SAC Combustor Design
text: NO concentration is computed in ppm from Eq. (110). The previous procedures are repeated to obtain the NO concentration in all three zones. Finally, the total NO ( total NO ) generated in the combustor is estimated using Eq. (111). The rate of NOx in g/s is obtained from Eq. (112). Design of a SAC using the developed Code The developed code has a user friendly interface as shown in Fig. 12. There are nine modules and each one runs by pressing a button. The structure of all modules is similar. The user interface of each module is divided into two portions: the upper portion is for the user input while the lower is for the program output. Fig. 12 shows the startup or

title: SAC Combustor Design
text: nine modules and each one runs by pressing a button. The structure of all modules is similar. The user interface of each module is divided into two portions: the upper portion is for the user input while the lower is for the program output. Fig. 12 shows the startup or the main page of the program. The user has to go through all the design modules in order. When button one is pressed the engine parameters module is loaded and the screen changes as shown in Fig. 13. The Engine parameters we discussed previously are entered and then the user proceeds with the design process. Fig. 13 is self explanatory. All the design modules work in a similar fashion. Fig. 14 shows the aerodynamic diffuser design. Fig. 15 shows the air distribution through the combustor. Fig. 16 shows the combustor sizing process. Fuel nozzle design is presented in Fig. 17. The axial swirlers design is shown in Fig. 18. Heat transfer calculations and temperatures estimation are shown in Fig. 19. Dilution holes design is shown in Fig. 20. Finally, the performance of the combustor is evaluated and shown in Fig. 21. The numerical values obtained from the developed code are practical and have good agreement with existing GTC.
